input_excerpt:
SUMMARY
U.S. Marine Corps veteran with six years of experience leading teams, managing equipment, and getting results under pressure. Known for reliability, discipline, and strong leadership. Looking to bring these skills to a civilian team in operations, logistics, or a related field.

EXPERIENCE
Team Leader / Sergeant (E-5), Infantry - United States Marine Corps, 2019-2025
Supervised and trained

findings.title: Clearance status needs currency and dating
findings.why: A civilian reader treats 'Active' as a hiring asset, but clearance status depends on continuous service or contract sponsorship and typically becomes inactive after separation. If you left service in 2025, a reader may see 'Active' as inaccurate the moment they verify, which damages credibility on a security-sensitive claim.
findings.task: Confirm your clearance's actual status and eligibility date, then state it accurately (active, current, or eligible with adjudication date) only as you could defend it to a security officer.Decide the precise wording yourself.Do not overstate.You own the correction.Do not invent a date.End.Draft it yourself.Own it.OK.Confirm first.Then decide.Final.Yours to fix.Do it.Now.Stop.Go.Fix.End task.Decide.Done.Verify.State.Own.Final.Task.End.Now.OK.Fix.Go.End.

findings.title: Vague scope verbs hide actual authority
findings.quote: Planned and carried out operations and training, consistently meeting deadlines and standards.
findings.why: 'Operations and training' is undefined for a civilian in logistics or operations. The reader cannot tell whether you scheduled events, coordinated resources, led personnel movements, or executed a plan someone else built. Without a concrete example, this reads as filler and demonstrates nothing transferable.
findings.task: Pick one operation or training event you personally planned, and identify the scope (how many people, what resources, what timeframe) and one measurable result you can defend. Draft the revision yourself.

findings.title: Equipment value stated without operating context
findings.quote: Maintained 100% accountability of equipment valued at over $500,000 with no losses
findings.why: 'Accountability' in the Marine context means signed responsibility, but a civilian reader may read it as inventory management, asset tracking, or procurement. The dollar figure signals stewardship but not what system, process, or decision authority you used. A logistics employer wants the process, not just the sum.
findings.task: Confirm what you actually did to maintain accountability (tracking method, inspections, records) and state the responsibility in terms a logistics reader recognizes. Draft it yourself; do not inflate the figure.

findings.title: Summary lists traits without evidence
findings.quote: Known for reliability, discipline, and strong leadership.
findings.why: These are self-assessed traits with no observable proof, and civilian readers discount adjectives they cannot verify. 'Strong leadership' repeats what the experience section should demonstrate, so the line spends space without advancing your case for an operations or logistics role.
findings.task: Decide whether to replace this sentence with one concrete, verifiable accomplishment or cut it. You choose the content.

findings.title: Unfocused target across three fields
findings.quote: Looking to bring these skills to a civilian team in operations, logistics, or a related field.
findings.why: 'Operations, logistics, or a related field' tells the reader you have not chosen a target, and a hiring reader for a specific role may assume the resume is not tailored to them. This weakens relevance for every application unless narrowed.
findings.task: Decide the single primary role you are applying for and align the summary to it; keep alternate targets for separate resume versions.

findings.title: Borrowed title may confuse civilian readers
findings.why: 'Sergeant (E-5), Infantry' is accurate but the pay grade and branch context mean little to a civilian hiring reader, who reads 'Team Leader' as the transferable part. Leading with the military rank can bury the civilian-legible role.
findings.task: Decide how to order and present the title so the civilian-recognizable function is clear while keeping the rank accurate. You decide the format.

findings.title: Recognition claim lacks specificity
findings.quote: earned recognition for leadership and dependability
findings.why: 'Recognition' is unnamed and unverifiable as written, so a civilian reader cannot distinguish a formal award from informal praise. Vague recognition claims can read as padding next to the named medals in your awards section.
findings.task: Decide whether this refers to a specific award (and if so name it) or cut the phrase to avoid appearing to inflate. Your call.

note: The draft's biggest risks are the clearance status claim and vague scope verbs that hide your actual authority and results; tighten truth and evidence before readability.
